personality_voice.book_baseline: The book corpus establishes Marvolo Gaunt as a violent, ignorant, obsessively proud pure-blood supremacist who lives in squalor while clinging to his ancestry as his only source of identity. Harry describes him as 'an ignorant old git who lived like a pig' who 'wasn't the type to read fairy tales to his kids' and whose entire self-worth derived from his bloodline. He physically intimidates Ministry officials (nearly shoving the ring in Ogden's face) and is characterized by aggression, contempt, and delusion.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: Marvolo Gaunt does not appear in the film corpus. The film screenplay provides zero scenes featuring him.
personality_voice.difference: The character is entirely absent from the films. No personality, voice, or behavioral traits are portrayed.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: A score of 0 is the only appropriate score when a character has no film presence whatsoever. There is nothing to evaluate faithfulness against.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Marvolo Gaunt serves a critical narrative function in HBP: he is the Pensieve-memory anchor that establishes Voldemort's maternal lineage, the Gaunt family's degradation, the origin of the locket Horcrux (via Merope), and crucially the ring Horcrux — which he brandishes before Ogden, establishing the Peverell coat of arms that later becomes the key to identifying the Deathly Hallows. His scene is the narrative mechanism by which Harry (and the reader) learns the ring's provenance.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: Absent. The Pensieve sequence showing the Gaunt family is not included in the films.
narrative_role_agency.difference: His entire narrative function — establishing Voldemort's origins, the ring's history, and the Peverell connection — is either cut or handled through other means in the films.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: Complete absence means complete loss of agency and narrative role. Score is 0.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Marvolo's motivations are clear from the book corpus: obsessive pride in pure-blood ancestry, contempt for Muggles and half-bloods, and a desperate clinging to the Gaunt name as compensation for material poverty. His internal conflict — if any — is the gap between his delusions of grandeur and his actual degraded circumstances.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: No motivations are conveyed in the films because the character does not appear.

character_arc.book_baseline: Marvolo's arc is essentially flat within the Pensieve memory — he is shown at a fixed point in time, already degraded, already imprisoned shortly after. His arc is less personal development and more a historical snapshot illustrating the Gaunt family's decline. The ring passes from him to Morfin to Voldemort.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Key relationships include his domineering, abusive dynamic with his children Morfin and Merope, and his hostile confrontation with Ogden (Ministry). These relationships reveal his cruelty, his contempt for Merope's attachment to a Muggle, and his role as the patriarch of a broken family that produces Voldemort's mother.
key_relationships.difference: None of these relationships are shown.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: The Gaunt family Pensieve scene is rich with complexity: the contrast between Marvolo's pride and his poverty, his casual cruelty toward Merope, the tragic irony that his obsession with blood purity produces a daughter who falls for a Muggle and a grandson who becomes the most feared dark wizard in history. The ring he brandishes as a status symbol is simultaneously a Deathly Hallow and a future Horcrux — layers of meaning he is entirely unaware of.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: All of this complexity is lost.

lost_or_transferred_material: The Pensieve memory of the Gaunt family home visit by Ogden is entirely cut from the films
Marvolo's establishment of Voldemort's maternal lineage and the Gaunt family's degradation is absent
The visual and narrative establishment of the ring as a Gaunt heirloom bearing the Peverell coat of arms is lost
The Peverell-to-Hallows connection that Harry makes in DH (triggered by his memory of Marvolo brandishing the ring) loses its direct visual anchor
The abusive family dynamic between Marvolo, Morfin, and Merope — which contextualizes Merope's desperation and Voldemort's origins — is absent
Marvolo's characterization as the embodiment of pure-blood supremacist delusion in poverty is entirely unshown
score_caps_applied: No caps needed — raw dimension scores already total 0 due to complete absence from the film corpus
comparative.key_observations: Marvolo Gaunt does not appear in the film corpus at all. The Pensieve sequences depicting the Gaunt family were cut from the film adaptations of Half-Blood Prince. As a result, faithfulness cannot be measured in any dimension — there is simply no film portrayal to evaluate. The score of 0 across all dimensions reflects absence, not poor adaptation quality. Confidence is Low across the board because the film corpus is empty. The most significant consequence of his omission is the loss of the narrative mechanism connecting the ring Horcrux to the Peverell lineage, which in the books is the key that allows Harry to identify the Deathly Hallows symbol in Deathly Hallows — a connection that the films handle differently or leave implicit.
